However, the CodeQL source is `httpServletRequest.getServletPath()`. In this test app, the JAX-RS application is mapped to `/*`, so `getServletPath()` is only the servlet-mapping portion of the URL (effectively fixed/empty here), not the user-controlled trailing path. The variable path segment is handled separately by JAX-RS as `@PathParam(\"path\")`, and that value is not written into the response. Therefore the reported source-to-sink flow does not yield exploitable reflected XSS. The finding is a false positive caused by selecting the wrong source for this deployment.",

Reported source at `SendUsernameServlet.java:203`: `output += httpServletRequest.getServletPath();`\n3. Under this mapping, `getServletPath()` is the servlet-mapping part, not the attacker-controlled remainder.\n4. The actual user-controlled path segment is captured in `SendUsernameServlet.java:132-135`: `public Response doGetElseWhere(@PathParam(\"path\") String path, ...) { ... return doGet(checkRolesFlag); }`. The `path` variable is only logged and is never appended to the HTML response.\n5. So even a request like `/%3Cscript%3Ealert(1)%3C/script%3E` would place the payload in `@PathParam(\"path\")`, not in the reflected `getServletPath()` value used by `getOutput()`.\n6. Additional real-world context: this code is under `testsuite/integration-arquillian/test-apps/servlets` and is deployed by `AbstractServletsAdapterTest.samlServletDeployment(...)` for integration tests, not as a production Keycloak endpoint.",

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The report points to a real unescaped HTML write inside `SendUsernameServlet`, but not to a real Keycloak product vulnerability. The sink is valid: `doGet()`/`doPost()` return `Response.ok(getOutput())` with `Content-Type: text/html`, and `getOutput()` concatenates `httpServletRequest.getServletPath()` directly into the response body without escaping. The control-flow path is also real inside this test app: `@GET @Path(\"{path}\")` in `doGetElseWhere()` delegates to `doGet()`, so a request to a subpath would reach the HTML response. There is no sanitizer on the path value. However, this class is located under `testsuite/integration-arquillian/test-apps/servlets` and is only used by Arquillian integration tests. It is packaged into temporary test WARs by the testsuite helper code and is not part of Keycloak's shipped/runtime server attack surface. So the finding is about vulnerable test harness code, not an exploitable endpoint in the real product deployment.",
  "FP_Reason": ["D4"],
  "evidence": "Source and sink in the reported file: lines 78 and 90 return `Response.ok(getOutput()).header(HttpHeaders.CONTENT_TYPE, MediaType.TEXT_HTML_TYPE + \";charset=UTF-8\")`; lines 201-204 build the body with `output += httpServletRequest.getServletPath();` and no escaping; lines 131-136 define `@GET @Path(\"{path}\")` and call `return doGet(checkRolesFlag);`, so the dynamic subpath route is reachable inside the test WAR. Deployment context: `testsuite/integration-arquillian/test-apps/servlets/pom.xml` declares artifactId `integration-arquillian-test-apps-servlets`; `testsuite/integration-arquillian/tests/base/src/test/java/org/keycloak/testsuite/adapter/AbstractServletsAdapterTest.java` builds test WARs with `samlServletDeployment(..., SendUsernameServlet.class)`; test classes such as `SAMLServletAdapterTest`, `SAMLLogoutAdapterTest`, and `SAMLLoginResponseHandlingTest` deploy it. This shows the servlet belongs to integration-test infrastructure only, not the shipped Keycloak runtime.",
  "new_reason:": "The false positive is caused by missing deployment-context awareness: the scanner flagged an integration-test application that is not part of the real product exposure. If this servlet were separately deployed, the XSS would be plausible, but that is outside Keycloak's real-world deployment scenario."

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The sink at SendUsernameServlet.java:78 is a real HTML sink because it returns `getOutput()` with `Content-Type: text/html`. If attacker-controlled HTML reached that response body, XSS would be possible. However, the specific CodeQL source in this report is line 203, `httpServletRequest.getServletPath()`, and that is the wrong source here. This class is used in the Arquillian test app module (`integration-arquillian-test-apps-servlets`), and the relevant test deployments map the JAX-RS application to `/*`. The attacker-controlled extra path segment is handled by `@Path(\"{path}\")` in `doGetElseWhere`/`doPostElseWhere`, but that `path` value is only logged and is not written into the response. The response body is built from `getServletPath()`, which in these deployments is mapping-derived/fixed (effectively the servlet mapping, not the arbitrary JAX-RS path remainder). So the reported source-to-sink flow does not reflect attacker-controlled input into the HTML response. This is therefore a false positive caused by incorrect source selection (B1), not by reachability or sanitizer issues.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "B1.Incorrect Source/Sink Selection"
  ],
  "evidence": "1. Sink: `return Response.ok(getOutput()).header(HttpHeaders.CONTENT_TYPE, MediaType.TEXT_HTML_TYPE + \";charset=UTF-8\").build();` at SendUsernameServlet.java:78.\n2. Reported source: `output += httpServletRequest.getServletPath();` at SendUsernameServlet.java:203.\n3. User path is not reflected: SendUsernameServlet.java:131-135 shows `@Path(\"{path}\") public Response doGetElseWhere(@PathParam(\"path\") String path, ...) { ... return doGet(checkRolesFlag); }` and lines 138-142 do the same for POST. The attacker-controlled `path` is only logged, not returned.\n4. The returned body is built in SendUsernameServlet.java:201-214 from `getServletPath()` and `principal.getName()`, not from `@PathParam(\"path\")`.\n5. Deployment mapping is fixed by test config: `adapter-test/keycloak-saml/web.xml` lines 26-33 map `jakarta.ws.rs.core.Application` to `/*`; the same `/*` mapping appears in the other web.xml variants packaging this class. Under that mapping, the arbitrary trailing request segment is the JAX-RS path remainder/path-param, not the servlet path used at line 203.\n6. Test-only context: testsuite/integration-arquillian/test-apps/servlets/pom.xml lines 5-13 identify this as artifact `integration-arquillian-test-apps-servlets` under `org.keycloak.testsuite`, so it is not production server code.",

  "report": "Rule Name: Cross-site scripting
Rule Description: Writing user input directly to a web page allows for a cross-site scripting vulnerability.
Severity: error
Detailed Message: Cross-site scripting vulnerability due to a [["user-provided value"|"relative:///services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/services/resources/LoginActionsService.java:443:41:443:108"]].
Location-file: /services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/protocol/saml/SamlProtocol.java
Location-start-line: 1113
Location-start-col: 28
Location-end-line: 1113
Location-end-col: 30",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The report is a false positive. The reported source is the `redirect_uri` query parameter in `LoginActionsService.resetCredentialsGET`, but that path creates an authentication session whose protocol is explicitly set to OIDC, not SAML. Keycloak later dispatches the post-authentication response by `authSession.getProtocol()`, so a session created from this source cannot execute `SamlProtocol.artifactPost`; the reported OIDC-to-SAML flow is not reachable at runtime. In the real reset-password scenario, the request either stays in the reset-credentials/account flow or finishes through the OIDC protocol handler, not through the SAML artifact POST page. As additional context, the accepted redirect URI is validated before being stored, and the SAML HTML form builder places data into escaped HTML-attribute context rather than raw HTML reflection.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "A2"
  ],
  "evidence": "1) Reported source: `LoginActionsService.resetCredentialsGET(..., @QueryParam(OIDCLoginProtocol.REDIRECT_URI_PARAM) String redirectUri, ...)` at `LoginActionsService.java:443`, and when there is no existing auth session it calls `createAuthenticationSessionForClient(clientId, redirectUri)` at `LoginActionsService.java:458`.\n2) In `createAuthenticationSessionForClient`, the value is first checked by `RedirectUtils.verifyRedirectUri(session, redirectUriParam, client)` (`LoginActionsService.java:487-490`), then the new authentication session is explicitly marked as OIDC: `authSession.setProtocol(OIDCLoginProtocol.LOGIN_PROTOCOL);` (`LoginActionsService.java:499`). If valid, the redirect is stored with `authSession.setRedirectUri(redirectUri);` (`LoginActionsService.java:503-505`).\n3) After authentication/required actions finish, Keycloak chooses the protocol implementation from that stored value: `LoginProtocol protocolImpl = session.getProvider(LoginProtocol.class, authSession.getProtocol());` (`AuthenticationManager.java:902`) and returns through that protocol (`AuthenticationManager.java:1073`). Therefore a session created from this source is handled by OIDC, not by `SamlProtocol`.\n4) The reported sink is in the SAML-only path. `SamlProtocol.authenticated(...)` reads `String redirectUri = authSession.getRedirectUri();` (`SamlProtocol.java:486`) and may call `artifactPost(...)` (`SamlProtocol.java:579-581`, `999-1000`), which returns `Response.ok(str, MediaType.TEXT_HTML_TYPE)` at `SamlProtocol.java:1113`. But SAML sessions are created separately in `SamlService.loginRequest`, where Keycloak sets `authSession.setProtocol(SamlProtocol.LOGIN_PROTOCOL);` and `authSession.setRedirectUri(redirect);` (`SamlService.java:481-482`). That is a different control-flow origin from the reported source.\n5) Secondary hardening: even inside the SAML form builder, the action URL and hidden input values are written via `escapeAttribute(actionUrl)` and `escapeAttribute(entry.getValue())` (`BaseSAML2BindingBuilder.java:418,423`), so the sink is not a raw direct write of attacker text into HTML markup.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

{
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The source is real user input (`redirect_uri` from `resetCredentialsGET`), but this report does not describe a reachable CWE-079 path. In this endpoint, the value is first validated by `RedirectUtils.verifyRedirectUri(...)` and then stored as redirect metadata in the authentication session. The only clear HTML rendering point for this value in the codebase is the `pageRedirectUri` link in `info.ftl`, but that branch is gated by auth-session notes that this reset-credentials path does not set. With a non-null `redirect_uri`, this flow normally completes via redirect/protocol handling, not by writing the value into an HTML page. So the reported source-to-sink path is not exploitable as XSS in the real execution of this endpoint.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "D3.Path-Insensitive Analysis"
  ],
  "evidence": "1) Source: `LoginActionsService.resetCredentialsGET(..., @QueryParam(OIDCLoginProtocol.REDIRECT_URI_PARAM) String redirectUri, ...)` at `services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/services/resources/LoginActionsService.java:439-445`.\n2) Validation/storage: `redirectUri = RedirectUtils.verifyRedirectUri(session, redirectUriParam, client); if (redirectUri == null) throw ...;` at `LoginActionsService.java:487-490`, then `authSession.setRedirectUri(redirectUri); authSession.setClientNote(..., redirectUri);` at `LoginActionsService.java:503-505`. `RedirectUtils.java:89-125` parses with `URI.create(...)`, requires a match against registered client redirect URIs, and rejects invalid/unapproved values.\n3) Potential HTML sink in repo: `themes/src/main/resources/theme/base/login/info.ftl:14-15` renders `<a href=\"${pageRedirectUri}\">...`.\n4) That sink is gated: `AuthenticationManager.finishedRequiredActions(...)` only sets `pageRedirectUri` when both `END_AFTER_REQUIRED_ACTIONS` and `SET_REDIRECT_URI_AFTER_REQUIRED_ACTIONS` are present (`services/src/main/java/org/keycloak/services/managers/AuthenticationManager.java:1046-1052`).\n5) Unreachable from this report path: `createAuthenticationSessionForClient(...)` sets `END_AFTER_REQUIRED_ACTIONS` only in the `redirectUri == null` branch (`LoginActionsService.java:503-508`), and this reset-credentials flow never sets `SET_REDIRECT_URI_AFTER_REQUIRED_ACTIONS`. Repo-wide setters of that flag are elsewhere only: `VerifyEmailActionTokenHandler.java:119-123`, `ExecuteActionsActionTokenHandler.java:100-106`, and `InviteOrgActionTokenHandler.java:146-151`.\n6) Other flow URLs do not carry raw `redirect_uri` into HTML; they use encoded `client_data`: `AuthenticationProcessor.getClientData(...)` returns `clientData.encode()` (`AuthenticationProcessor.java:307-310`), and `ClientData.encode()` Base64-encodes the JSON (`server-spi-private/src/main/java/org/keycloak/protocol/ClientData.java:108-110`).",
  "new_reason:": ""
}",

  "analysis": "This specific CodeQL report is a false positive. The sink is real: `doPost()` returns `Response.ok(getOutput())` with `Content-Type: text/html`, so if attacker-controlled HTML reached `getOutput()`, XSS would be possible. However, the reported source is not actually attacker-controlled in this deployment. The flagged value is `httpServletRequest.getServletPath()` in `getOutput()` (line 203), while the routable attacker-controlled segment is the JAX-RS `@PathParam(\"path\")` handled by `doPostElseWhere()`. That `path` value is only written to `System.out` and is never inserted into the HTTP response. Because the application is mapped in `web.xml` to `/*`, the trailing resource segment is handled as JAX-RS path info/resource matching, not as a reflected servlet-path string used here. So the chosen source for this alert is incorrect for the actual runtime behavior.",

The response body is produced in `doPost()` by `Response.ok(getOutput()).header(HttpHeaders.CONTENT_TYPE, MediaType.TEXT_HTML_TYPE + \";charset=UTF-8\")`. Inside `getOutput()`, the reflected value is `httpServletRequest.getServletPath()` (line 203), not the `@PathParam(\"path\")`. Deployment config `testsuite/integration-arquillian/tests/base/src/test/resources/adapter-test/keycloak-saml/web.xml` maps `jakarta.ws.rs.core.Application` to `/*`, so the extra URL segment matched by `@Path(\"{path}\")` is separate routing data and is not the value written by `getServletPath()` here.
